g.data-narration: Welcome to DomainKeys Identified Mail - the essential email authentication standard that prevents spoofing and phishing attacks.
DKIM is a method that uses public-key cryptography to verify that an email was sent by the claimed domain and wasn't tampered with in transit.
g.text: DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM) is a cryptographic method for
authenticating email messages. It uses public-key infrastructure (PKI)
to verify sender identity and message integrity.

g.data-narration: DKIM prevents email spoofing - a technique attackers use to send fake emails that appear to come from legitimate domains.
g.text: • Prevents email spoofing - attackers fake sender addresses
• Blocks phishing attacks - fake login pages and malware delivery
• Improves email deliverability - major providers require DKIM

g.data-narration: DKIM uses a three-component system: the signing server with private key, DNS to publish the public key, and verification at the receiver.
The domain owner generates an RSA key pair - keeping the private key secret on their mail server, and publishing the public key in DNS TXT records.
The signing process creates a hash of email headers and body, then encrypts it with the private key - this becomes the DKIM signature.
The DKIM signature header contains the selector, domain, and the Base64-encoded signature - all critical for verification.
The receiver extracts the selector from the DKIM header, looks up the public key in DNS, and verifies the signature using RSA.
DKIM uses SHA-256 for hashing and RSA for encryption - the combination ensures both integrity and authenticity of email messages.
g.text: Sign: Signature = RSA( SHA-256(Headers + Body), PrivateKey )
Verify: SHA-256(Headers + Body) ?= RSA( Signature, PublicKey )

g.data-narration: The selector allows multiple DKIM keys per domain - enabling key rotation and different keys for subdomains or email services.
g.text: The selector is a unique string that identifies which DKIM key to use for verification.

g.data-narration: DKIM provides authentication, reduces spam and phishing, improves email deliverability, and enables compliance with security standards.
Common DKIM problems include incorrect key configuration, missing DNS records, selector errors, and key expiration without rotation.
Best practices include using 2048-bit+ keys, regular key rotation, unique selectors per service, and testing with DKIM validators.
DKIM is essential for email security - it verifies sender authenticity, prevents spoofing, and works with SPF and DMARC for comprehensive protection.
g.text: Conclusion: DKIM + SPF + DMARC = Complete Email Security
